transcript: [doctor] tyler nelson , date of birth 3/6/1949 , mrn 385729 . he is here today for ongoing management of rheumatoid arthritis . last time he was here he received a steroid injection in his right knee . hello mr. nelson , how are you ? [patient] about the same as last time . my knee is still swollen . [doctor] that was your right knee , correct ? i take it the injection , did it help ? [patient] it did maybe for a couple of weeks . [doctor] that's it and is it still painful ? [patient] yeah , it's pretty painful and now the whole leg is getting swollen . [doctor] okay . what would you say your pain level is ? [patient] i mean right now it's about a three or so but typically on the normal day , it's around six . [doctor] okay and it's been swollen like that for how long now ? [patient] since i was last here a couple of weeks after that , so about maybe 2 months now . [doctor] okay , but before it was just the knee ? [patient] yeah , the whole thing was n't swollen like this , but i tried to wear compression socks a lot of the time but it has n't been helping . [doctor] okay . okay . so that has n't been working for you . [patient] i mean it helps to some degree but it's still swollen especially around the knee . [doctor] hmmm has it been keeping you from doing things during the day ? [patient] well i slowed down a little bit and i try to do things too fast but when it really hurts is when i've been sitting for a while and then i try to get up . i'm so stiff getting up , i have to use a cane to get my bearings i mean after a little bit it loosens up but at first getting up is really difficult . [doctor] yeah , i can see , that makes sense . have you been taking your medication regularly ? you're on xeljanz , right ? [patient] yeah , the xr i think it's 11 mg a day , yeah , but i'm taking it everyday and i'm also taking celebrex , which i started maybe 2 months ago . [doctor] yeah , the 200 mix daily . how is that working for you ? [patient] pretty good up until this started swelling again . [doctor] okay well come sit over here and if you could take off your socks and shoes and we will take a look at your knee . and just to let you know , i have a service now that writes my note about the visit so you'll hear , hear me call things out and describe what i see fully . okay , gotcha . [patient] right , so let's check your leg , can you straighten it and does it hurt ? yeah , when i try to straighten it all the way , it does . [doctor] okay . range of motion slightly diminished . i'm going to press here , okay ? and how about here ? hmmm i see some pitting around the knee and ankle . normal capillary refill in the toes . does any of this area hurt ? [patient] just like an old bruise . [doctor] so i can feel that the knee is hot and that means there is something going on like some inflammation so we can give you another steroid injection for that . i also want to get an x-ray of that knee so we can see where all the swelling is coming from . yeah sounds good to me . [patient] okay and do we set up physical therapy for you last time you were here ? [doctor] no i was doing some years ago , but that was the other knee . okay so i think we will set that up for you now . we will also do an x-ray on that knee i can send you for a physical therapy and if nothing is helping then we really might need to get a surgery consult to see what we can do with that knee . [patient] okay . sounds like a plan . [doctor] alright , so for today , we'll go ahead and we'll give you the steroid shot . i'll try to see if i can get a little bit of fluid out before i do give you the injection . there might be no fluid , but i'll see if i can get anything out regardless . [patient] okay and that's the procedure you're gon na do right now ? [doctor] yep once we're done talking here and as far as the edema in your legs , we'll take an x-ray to look for the cause , but there is a lot you can . do as well . the compression socks are a good start and also do you eat a lot of salt ? [patient] i mean i , i mean i love my salty snacks . [doctor] okay . well salt does have a tendency to make your body hold on to water , reducing your salt intake could help with some of the swelling . you can also prop your feet up anytime you get a chance especially at night . [patient] and have you talked to your primary care doctor about this at all ? no , i think i would just come here first . okay . so i think try some of those things out first and if it does n't help , definitely make an appointment with them . [doctor] also as you know xeljanz is a high risk medication , so we need to check your cmp levels every couple of months so this time your levels are good but you make sure to make an appointment for the blood work for next time . [patient] alright i can do that . thank you so much doc . [doctor] yes of course well let me go ahead and get supplies and we'll get to work on your knee . fluid aspiration and steroid injection procedure . the risks , benefits , and alternatives were explained . the risks of steroid injection were explained including but not limited to hypopigmentation , soft tissue atrophy , tendon ruptures and transient hyperglycemia among others . verbal consent was obtained . the right knee was accessed through the lateral approach with 3 ml of initially clear fluid , then blood . blood tinged fluid . then the right knee was prepped in a standard fashion . the right knee was injected with 80 mg of triamcin alone . and 1 ml of lidocaine . the procedure was tolerated well with no complications . post procedure instructions were given . patient will follow up with his pcp . thank you .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Rheumatoid arthritis management.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Nelson is a 72-year-old male who presents today for ongoing management of rheumatoid arthritis. He was last seen by me on 03/04/2021, where he received a steroid injection to his right knee. He continues to experience right knee pain and swelling. The pain level is 6/10. He notes when he stands after he has been sitting for a while, he has to use a cane to "get his bearings." The injection he received to the right knee at the last visit was only beneficial for a few weeks. The patient notes whole right leg swelling since the last visit. He usually wears support hose, which helps to some degree. He has not attended physical therapy.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Musculoskeletal
Lower right extremity: Swollen right knee, slightly reduced range of motion. Skin warm to touch. Lower right leg pits with pressure.

PROCEDURE

Fluid aspiration and Steroid injection.
Risks, benefits, and alternatives were explained. The risks of steroid injection were explained, including but not limited to, hypopigmentation, soft tissue atrophy, tendon ruptures, and transient hyperglycemia among others. Verbal consent was obtained. The right knee was accessed through the lateral approach with 3 mL of initially clear fluid then blood-tinged fluid. Then the right knee was prepped in a standard fashion. The right knee was injected with 80 mg triamcinolone and 1 mL lidocaine. The procedure was tolerated well with no complications. Post procedure instructions were given.

ASSESSMENT

 Rheumatoid arthritis management.
 High risk medication use.
 Right knee pain.
 Lower extremity pitting edema.

PLAN

Rheumatoid arthritis management.
The patient will continue Xeljanz XR 11 mg daily. He will also continue Celebrex 200 mg daily.

High-risk medication use.
The patient is currently on the high-risk medication Xeljanz. We will check his CMP levels and monitor periodically every couple of months.

Right knee pain.
He has right knee pain and swelling, with pain level 6/10. On exam today, the right knee continues to be hot, which is ongoing inflammation. We performed a fluid aspiration of the right knee today, see procedure note above. A steroid injection to the right knee was given today, see procedure note above. We will arrange for x-ray of the right knee and refer his to physical therapy.

Lower extremity pitting edema.
The patient notes whole right leg swelling since the last visit. He usually wears support hose, which helps to some degree. He has not seen his PCP. We discussed compression stockings, elevate feet, and a low salt diet. He will check with his PCP for further instructions.
notes:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Mr. Nelson, a 73-year-old male, presents with ongoing management of rheumatoid arthritis. He reports that his right knee has been swollen for approximately 2 months, with pain level ranging from 3 to 6 out of 10. He has tried compression socks, which provide some relief but not complete resolution. He experiences stiffness, particularly when getting up from a seated position, and requires a cane for support. He has been taking Xeljanz (11 mg daily) and Celebrex (200 mg daily) for 2 months, with some improvement in symptoms before the recent increase in swelling.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, for which he has been receiving treatment.

Review of systems: The patient reports no other symptoms related to his rheumatoid arthritis.

Current medications:
Xeljanz 11 mg daily
Celebrex 200 mg daily

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The patient's knee is swollen, with pitting around the knee and ankle. The patient reports pain and stiffness, particularly when straightening the knee. Normal capillary refill in the toes.

Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has recurrent swelling and pain in his right knee, likely related to his rheumatoid arthritis.

Problem list: Rheumatoid arthritis; swelling of the right knee; pain of the right knee; stiffness of the right knee.

Plan:
I prescribed a steroid injection (80 mg of triamcinolone and 1 ml of lidocaine) for the patient's right knee. I also ordered an x-ray of the knee to investigate the cause of the swelling. I will refer the patient to physical therapy to address his stiffness and mobility issues. If the current treatment plan does not improve his symptoms, I will consider a surgery consult to discuss further management options.

Follow-up: I will see the patient again in 2-3 months to reassess his symptoms and adjust his treatment plan as needed. I also instructed the patient to follow up with his primary care physician for routine monitoring of his CMP levels, which are currently within normal limits.

Subjective:
The patient, Mr. Nelson, is a 72-year-old male presenting for ongoing management of rheumatoid arthritis. He reports his knee is still swollen and painful, with a pain level of approximately 3 out of 10 on a normal day, but up to 6 out of 10 at times. The swelling has been present for about 2 months, and he has tried using compression socks, which provide some relief but are not fully effective. He has difficulty getting up from sitting, requiring a cane for support, and experiences stiffness in his knee. He takes Xeljanz 11 mg daily and Celebrex 200 mg daily, which he reports has been effective until the swelling recurred.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, for which he has been receiving treatment. He has also been taking Xeljanz and Celebrex as mentioned above.

Review of systems: The patient reports no other symptoms in other organs.

Current medications:
Xeljanz 11 mg daily
Celebrex 200 mg daily

Objective:

Physical exam: The patient's knee is swollen, with pitting around the knee and ankle, but normal capillary refill in the toes. The knee is hot to the touch, indicating inflammation.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has ongoing rheumatoid arthritis with worsening knee swelling and pain.
Problem list: Rheumatoid Arthritis; Swelling of the knee; Pain; Inflammation of the knee.

Plan:
I prescribed a steroid injection of 80 mg of triamcinolone and 1 ml of lidocaine for the knee inflammation. I also ordered an x-ray of the knee to investigate the cause of the swelling. I recommended the patient try reducing their salt intake and propping their feet up at night to alleviate swelling. I advised the patient to follow up with their primary care physician to discuss further management of their condition. I also instructed the patient to schedule blood work for CPM levels, as Xeljanz is a high-risk medication.

Follow-up: The patient will follow up with his primary care physician and return for a follow-up appointment as needed.
